name.text: CSIR NET JUNE 2019 Part C Q61

questiontext.text:
Let \(\{a_n\}_{n\ge0}\) be positive reals and \(K=\limsup_{n\to\infty}|a_n|^{1/n}\). Which are true?

  1. If \(K=\infty\), then \(\sum a_n r^n\) converges for every \(r>0\)
  2. If \(K=\infty\), then \(\sum a_n r^n\) does not converge for any \(r>0\)
  3. If \(K=0\), then \(\sum a_n r^n\) converges for every \(r>0\)
  4. If \(K=0\), then \(\sum a_n r^n\) does not converge for any \(r>0\)

generalfeedback.text: Answer: Options 2 and 3. The radius of convergence is \(R=1/K\). If \(K=\infty\), \(R=0\) → series diverges for all \(r>0\) (opt 2 correct). If \(K=0\), \(R=\infty\) → series converges for all \(r>0\) (opt 3 correct).

answer.text: If \(K=\infty\), \(\sum a_n r^n\) does not converge for any \(r>0\)
If \(K=0\), \(\sum a_n r^n\) does not converge for any \(r>0\)

name.text: CSIR NET JUNE 2019 Part C Q62

questiontext.text:
Define \(d:\mathbb{R}\times\mathbb{R}\to[0,\infty)\) by \(d(x,y)=|\lfloor x\rfloor-\lfloor y\rfloor|\). Which are true?

  1. \(d(x,y)=0\Leftrightarrow x=y\)
  2. \(d(x,y)=d(y,x)\)
  3. \(d(x,y)\le d(x,z)+d(z,y)\)
  4. \(d\) is not a metric on \(\mathbb{R}\)

generalfeedback.text: Answer: Options 2 and 4. Opt 1 false: \(d(0.1,0.5)=|0-0|=0\) but \(0.1\ne0.5\). Opt 2 true: absolute value is symmetric. Opt 3: the triangle inequality does hold for \(d\), but opt 4 is also true since \(d(x,y)=0\not\Rightarrow x=y\). Official key: opts 2 and 4.

feedback.text: Incorrect per official key (though triangle inequality does hold, the key selects opts 2 and 4).

name.text: CSIR NET JUNE 2019 Part C Q63

questiontext.text:
Consider \(f:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}\). Which are true?

  1. \(f\) is not one-one if its graph meets some horizontal line in \(\ge2\) points
  2. \(f\) is one-one if its graph meets every horizontal line in \(\le1\) point
  3. \(f\) is surjective if its graph meets every horizontal line
  4. \(f\) is not surjective if its graph misses at least one horizontal line

generalfeedback.text: Answer: All four options. All four statements are direct logical consequences of the definitions of injectivity and surjectivity in terms of horizontal line tests.

answer.text: \(f\) not one-one if graph meets some horizontal line \(\ge2\) times
\(f\) one-one if graph meets every horizontal line \(\le1\) time
\(f\) surjective if graph meets every horizontal line
\(f\) not surjective if graph misses at least one horizontal line

name.text: CSIR NET JUNE 2019 Part C Q64

questiontext.text:
Let \(f(x)=\int_1^\infty\frac{\cos t}{x^2+t^2}\,dt\). Which are true?

  1. \(f\) is bounded on \(\mathbb{R}\)
  2. \(f\) is continuous on \(\mathbb{R}\)
  3. \(f\) is not defined everywhere on \(\mathbb{R}\)
  4. \(f\) is not continuous on \(\mathbb{R}\)

generalfeedback.text: Answer: Options 1 and 2. \(|f(x)|\le\int_1^\infty\frac{dt}{x^2+t^2}\le\int_1^\infty\frac{dt}{t^2}=1\) (bounded). The integrand is uniformly continuous in \(x\) (dominated convergence), so \(f\) is continuous. \(f\) is defined for all \(x\in\mathbb{R}\) since the integral converges absolutely.

name.text: CSIR NET JUNE 2019 Part C Q65

questiontext.text:
Let \(\{x_n\}\) be positive reals, \(y_n=\frac{x_n}{1+x_n}\). Which are true?

  1. \(\{x_n\}\) converges if \(\{y_n\}\) converges
  2. \(\{y_n\}\) converges if \(\{x_n\}\) converges
  3. \(\{y_n\}\) is bounded if \(\{x_n\}\) is bounded
  4. \(\{x_n\}\) is bounded if \(\{y_n\}\) is bounded

generalfeedback.text: Answer: Options 2 and 3. Opt 2: \(y_n=f(x_n)\) with \(f\) continuous → convergence passes. Opt 3: \(y_n<1\) always → bounded. Opt 1 fails: if \(y_n\to1\), \(x_n\to\infty\). Opt 4 fails: \(\{y_n\}\) is always bounded (below 1), even when \(x_n=n\to\infty\).

feedback.text: Incorrect — \(\{y_n\}\) is always bounded but \(\{x_n\}\) need not be.

name.text: CSIR NET JUNE 2019 Part C Q66

questiontext.text:
Let \(f(x)=x\sin(1/x)\) for \(x\in(0,1]\), \(f(0)=0\), and \(g(x)=xf(x)=x^2\sin(1/x)\). Which are true?

  1. \(f\) is of bounded variation on \([0,1]\)
  2. \(f\) is not of bounded variation on \([0,1]\)
  3. \(g\) is of bounded variation on \([0,1]\)
  4. \(g\) is not of bounded variation on \([0,1]\)

generalfeedback.text: Answer: Options 2 and 3. \(f(x)=x\sin(1/x)\): the oscillations near 0 are of order \(1/n\) with infinitely many sign changes — total variation diverges → NOT BV (opt 2). \(g(x)=x^2\sin(1/x)\): \(|g'(x)|=|2x\sin(1/x)-\cos(1/x)|\le3\) is bounded → \(g\) is Lipschitz → BV (opt 3).

name.text: CSIR NET JUNE 2019 Part C Q67

questiontext.text:
Let \(a<c<b\), \(f:(a,b)\to\mathbb{R}\) continuous, differentiable on \((a,b)\setminus\{c\}\), and \(\lim_{x\to c}f'(x)=L\) exists. Which are true?

  1. \(f\) is differentiable at \(c\)
  2. \(f\) need not be differentiable at \(c\)
  3. \(f\) is differentiable at \(c\) and \(\lim_{x\to c}f'(x)=f'(c)\)
  4. \(f\) is differentiable at \(c\) but \(f'(c)\) need not equal \(\lim_{x\to c}f'(x)\)

generalfeedback.text: Answer: Options 1 and 3. By the MVT: \(\frac{f(x)-f(c)}{x-c}=f'(\xi)\) for some \(\xi\) between \(x\) and \(c\). As \(x\to c\), \(\xi\to c\), so \(f'(\xi)\to L\). Thus \(f'(c)=L=\lim_{x\to c}f'(x)\). Both opts 1 and 3 hold.

answer.text: \(f\) differentiable at \(c\) but \(f'(c)\) need not equal the limit

name.text: CSIR NET JUNE 2019 Part C Q68

questiontext.text:
Let \(F:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}\) be non-decreasing. Which of the following can be the set of discontinuities of \(F\)?

  1. \(\mathbb{Z}\)
  2. \(\mathbb{N}\)
  3. \(\mathbb{Q}\)
  4. \(\mathbb{R}\setminus\mathbb{Q}\)

generalfeedback.text: Answer: Options 1, 2, and 3. A monotone function can have at most countably many discontinuities (all jump discontinuities). \(\mathbb{Z}\) and \(\mathbb{N}\) are countable → possible. \(\mathbb{Q}\) is countable → possible (e.g., enumerate rationals and assign jumps). \(\mathbb{R}\setminus\mathbb{Q}\) is uncountable → NOT possible.

feedback.text: Incorrect — uncountable, impossible for a monotone function.

name.text: CSIR NET JUNE 2019 Part C Q69

questiontext.text:
Let \(f(x_1,x_2,x_3)=(e^{x_2}\cos x_1, e^{x_2}\sin x_1, 2x_1-\cos x_3)\). Let \(E\) be the set of points around which \(f\) is locally an open map. Which are true?

  1. \(E=\mathbb{R}^3\)
  2. \(E\) is countable
  3. \(E\) is uncountable but not \(\mathbb{R}^3\)
  4. \(\{(x_1,x_2,\pi/2):x_1,x_2\in\mathbb{R}\}\subsetneq E\)

generalfeedback.text: Answer: Options 3 and 4. Jacobian determinant: \(\det J=-e^{2x_2}\sin x_3\). This is nonzero iff \(\sin x_3 e0\), i.e., \(x_3 e k\pi\). By IFT, \(f\) is locally open where \(\det J e0\). So \(E=\{(x_1,x_2,x_3):\sin x_3 e0\}\), which is \(\mathbb{R}^3\) minus countably many planes — uncountable but not all of \(\mathbb{R}^3\) (opt 3). At \(x_3=\pi/2\), \(\sin(\pi/2)=1 e0\), so the plane \(\{x_3=\pi/2\}\subset E\) (opt 4).

name.text: CSIR NET JUNE 2019 Part C Q70

questiontext.text:
Let \(X\) be a countable set. Which are true?

  1. There exists a metric \(d\) on \(X\) s.t. \((X,d)\) is complete
  2. There exists a metric \(d\) on \(X\) s.t. \((X,d)\) is not complete
  3. There exists a metric \(d\) on \(X\) s.t. \((X,d)\) is compact
  4. There exists a metric \(d\) on \(X\) s.t. \((X,d)\) is not compact

generalfeedback.text: Answer: Options 1, 2, 3, 4 (all, per official key; or 1 and 3 for general countable). Opt 1: discrete metric → complete. Opt 3: map \(X\) bijectively to \(\{0\}\cup\{1/n\}\subset\mathbb{R}\) → compact. For countably infinite \(X\): Opt 2: map to \(\mathbb{Q}\) with usual metric → not complete. Opt 4: discrete metric on infinite \(X\) → not compact. Official key accepts all four.

name.text: CSIR NET JUNE 2019 Part C Q71

questiontext.text:
Let \(L(\mathbb{R}^n)\) be \(\mathbb{R}\)-linear maps \(\mathbb{R}^n\to\mathbb{R}^n\). Which are true?

  1. There exists \(T\in L(\mathbb{R}^5)\setminus\{0\}\) with Range\((T)=\ker(T)\)
  2. There does not exist such \(T\) in \(L(\mathbb{R}^5)\)
  3. There exists \(T\in L(\mathbb{R}^6)\setminus\{0\}\) with Range\((T)=\ker(T)\)
  4. There does not exist such \(T\) in \(L(\mathbb{R}^6)\)

generalfeedback.text: Answer: Options 2 and 3. Range\((T)=\ker(T)\) requires dim Range + dim ker = \(n\) and 2 dim Range = \(n\) → \(n\) must be even. For \(n=5\) (odd): impossible (opt 2). For \(n=6\): take \(T\) that maps onto a 3-dim subspace equal to its kernel (e.g., standard nilpotent block) → possible (opt 3).

name.text: CSIR NET JUNE 2019 Part C Q72

questiontext.text:
For finite-dim \(V\) and \(T:V\to V\) linear, can we always write \(T=T_2\circ T_1\) with \(T_1:V\to W\), \(T_2:W\to V\) such that:

  1. both \(T_1,T_2\) are onto
  2. both are one-to-one
  3. \(T_1\) is onto, \(T_2\) is one-to-one
  4. \(T_1\) is one-to-one, \(T_2\) is onto

generalfeedback.text: Answer: Options 3 and 4. Let \(W=\)Range\((T)\). For opt 3: \(T_1:V\to W\) surjective (factor \(T\) onto its image, \(T_1(v)=T(v)\in W\)) and \(T_2:W\hookrightarrow V\) the inclusion (injective). For opt 4: \(T_1:V\to\ker T^\perp\) projection (injective on complement) and \(T_2:\ker T^\perp\to V\) surjective. Opts 1,2 fail for non-invertible \(T\).

name.text: CSIR NET JUNE 2019 Part C Q73

questiontext.text:
Let \(A=(a_{ij})\) be a \(3\times3\) complex matrix. Which are correct?

  1. \(\det((-1)^{i+j}a_{ij})=\det A\)
  2. \(\det((-1)^{i+j}a_{ij})=-\det A\)
  3. \(\det((\sqrt{-1})^{i+j}a_{ij})=\det A\)
  4. \(\det((\sqrt{-1})^{i+j}a_{ij})=-\det A\)

generalfeedback.text: Answer: Options 1 and 3. Let \(B_{ij}=(-1)^{i+j}a_{ij}=D_i a_{ij} D_j\) where \(D=\mathrm{diag}(-1,-1,-1)\) scaled... more precisely: \(B=\mathrm{diag}(-1,1,-1)\cdot A\cdot\mathrm{diag}(-1,1,-1)\) for \(3\times3\). \(\det B=(-1)^3\cdot(-1)^3\det A=\det A\) (opt 1). For \(C_{ij}=i^{i+j}a_{ij}\): similarly \(\det C=i^{\sum(i+j)}\) scaled... computing: \(\sum_{i,j}(i+j)\) over a permutation... actually the factor is \(\prod_{i=1}^3 i^{?}\). By similar diagonal argument: \(\det C=i^{2\cdot(1+2+3)}\det A=i^{12}\det A=(i^4)^3\det A=1\cdot\det A=\det A\) (opt 3).

name.text: CSIR NET JUNE 2019 Part C Q74

questiontext.text:
Let \(p(x)\) be a non-constant polynomial of degree \(n\), \(q(x)=\int_0^x p(t)\,dt\), \(r(x)=p'(x)\). In the vector space \(V\) of all polynomials, which are true?

  1. \(q\) and \(r\) are linearly independent in \(V\)
  2. \(q\) and \(r\) are linearly dependent in \(V\)
  3. \(x^n\) belongs to the linear span of \(q\) and \(r\)
  4. \(x^{n+1}\) belongs to the linear span of \(q\) and \(r\)

generalfeedback.text: Answer: Option 1. \(\deg q=n+1\) and \(\deg r=n-1\). Since their degrees differ, they cannot be scalar multiples of each other → linearly independent (opt 1). Span of \(q,r\) consists of polynomials; \(x^n\) has degree \(n\), which is between \(n-1\) and \(n+1\) but not in span in general. Opt 4: \(x^{n+1}\) could equal \(q\) (which has degree \(n+1\)) only if \(q=x^{n+1}\), not generally true.

name.text: CSIR NET JUNE 2019 Part C Q75

questiontext.text:
Let \(M_n(\mathbb{R})\) be the ring of \(n\times n\) real matrices (\(n\ge2\)). Which are true for every \(n\ge2\)?

  1. There exist \(A,B\in M_n(\mathbb{R})\) with \(AB-BA=I_n\)
  2. If \(AB=BA\), then \(A\) diagonalizable \(\Leftrightarrow\) \(B\) diagonalizable
  3. If \(A,B\in M_n(\mathbb{R})\), then \(AB\) and \(BA\) have the same minimal polynomial
  4. If \(A,B\in M_n(\mathbb{R})\), then \(AB\) and \(BA\) have the same real eigenvalues

generalfeedback.text: Answer: Option 4. Opt 1 is false: \(\mathrm{tr}(AB-BA)=0\ne\mathrm{tr}(I_n)=n\). Opt 2 is false: \(A=\begin{pmatrix}1&1\\0&1\end{pmatrix}\), \(B=I\): commute; \(B\) is diagonalizable, \(A\) is not. Opt 3 is false (minimal polynomials can differ). Opt 4: The nonzero eigenvalues of \(AB\) and \(BA\) coincide (standard result), and 0 is an eigenvalue of \(AB\) iff it is of \(BA\) (since their characteristic polynomials differ by at most a factor of \(\lambda^{|m-n|}\)). Hence same real eigenvalues.

answer.text: \(AB\) and \(BA\) have the same minimal polynomial
\(AB\) and \(BA\) have the same real eigenvalues

name.text: CSIR NET JUNE 2019 Part C Q76

questiontext.text:
Let \(A=(a_{ij})_{5\times5}\) with \(a_{ij}=\frac{1}{n_i+n_j+1}\) where \(n_i,n_j\in\mathbb{N}\). In which cases is \(A\) positive definite?

  1. \(n_i=i\) for \(i=1,\ldots,5\)
  2. \(n_1<n_2<\cdots<n_5\)
  3. \(n_1=n_2=\cdots=n_5\)
  4. \(n_1>n_2>\cdots>n_5\)

generalfeedback.text: Answer: Options 1, 2, and 4. The matrix \(a_{ij}=\frac{1}{m_i+m_j+1}\) is the Cauchy matrix and is positive definite iff all \(m_i\) are distinct (here \(m_i=n_i\)). Opts 1,2,4 give distinct \(n_i\); opt 3 gives all equal \(n_i\) → matrix is rank 1 → not positive definite.

feedback.text: Incorrect — all equal → rank 1 → not positive definite.

name.text: CSIR NET JUNE 2019 Part C Q77

questiontext.text:
For nonzero \(w\in\mathbb{R}^n\), define \(T_w(v)=v-\frac{2\langle v,w\rangle}{\langle w,w\rangle}w\) (Householder reflection). Which are true?

  1. \(\det(T_w)=1\)
  2. \(\langle T_w(v_1),T_w(v_2)\rangle=\langle v_1,v_2\rangle\) for all \(v_1,v_2\)
  3. \(T_w=T_w^{-1}\)
  4. \(T_{2w}=2T_w\)

generalfeedback.text: Answer: Options 2 and 3. \(T_w\) is an orthogonal reflection: it preserves inner products (opt 2). \(T_w^2=I\) so \(T_w^{-1}=T_w\) (opt 3). \(\det T_w=-1\) (reflection) so opt 1 is false. Opt 4: \(T_{2w}=T_w\) (the same reflection) ≠ \(2T_w\).

name.text: CSIR NET JUNE 2019 Part C Q78

questiontext.text:
Consider \(A=\begin{pmatrix}0&1\\1&0\end{pmatrix}\) over \(\mathbb{Q}\). Which matrices are of the form \(P^TAP\) for some invertible \(P\) over \(\mathbb{Q}\)?

  1. \(\begin{pmatrix}2&0\\0&-2\end{pmatrix}\)
  2. \(\begin{pmatrix}2&0\\0&2\end{pmatrix}\)
  3. \(\begin{pmatrix}1&0\\0&-1\end{pmatrix}\)
  4. \(\begin{pmatrix}3&4\\4&5\end{pmatrix}\)

generalfeedback.text: Answer: Options 1, 3, and 4. \(A\) is the symmetric bilinear form \(x_1x_2+x_2x_1=2x_1x_2\) with discriminant \(-1\). Options sharing the same discriminant/Witt index over \(\mathbb{Q}\) are congruent to \(A\). Opt 2: \(\begin{pmatrix}2&0\\0&2\end{pmatrix}\) has discriminant \(4>0\) (two positive eigenvalues) → not congruent to \(A\) (which is indefinite). Opts 1,3,4 are all indefinite over \(\mathbb{Q}\) and congruent to \(A\).

feedback.text: Incorrect — positive definite, not congruent to indefinite \(A\).

name.text: CSIR NET JUNE 2019 Part C Q79

questiontext.text:
Let \(f(z)=(z^3+1)\sin z^2=u(x,y)+iv(x,y)\). Which are true?

  1. \(u:\mathbb{R}^2\to\mathbb{R}\) is infinitely differentiable
  2. \(u\) is continuous but not differentiable
  3. \(u\) is bounded
  4. For all \(z\in\mathbb{C}\), \(f\) can be represented by an absolutely convergent power series \(\sum a_n z^n\)

generalfeedback.text: Answer: Options 1 and 4. \(f(z)=(z^3+1)\sin z^2\) is entire. The real part \(u\) of an entire function is \(C^\infty\) (harmonic, hence real analytic) → opt 1. \(f\) is entire so has an everywhere-convergent Taylor series → opt 4. \(u\) is unbounded (entire non-constant) → opt 3 false. \(u\) is \(C^\infty\) → opt 2 false.

answer.text: \(f\) has an absolutely convergent power series for all \(z\)

name.text: CSIR NET JUNE 2019 Part C Q80

questiontext.text:
Let \(\Omega=\{z\in\mathbb{C}:\mathrm{Re}(z)>|\mathrm{Im}(z)|\}\) and \(f_n(z)=\log z^n\) (principal branch). Which are true?

  1. \(f_1(\Omega)=\{z:0\le|\mathrm{Im}(z)|<\pi/4\}\)
  2. \(f_2(\Omega)=\{z:0\le|\mathrm{Im}(z)|<\pi/2\}\)
  3. \(f_3(\Omega)=\{z:0\le|\mathrm{Im}(z)|<3\pi/4\}\)
  4. \(f_4(\Omega)=\{z:0\le|\mathrm{Im}(z)|<\pi\}\)

generalfeedback.text: Answer: Options 1, 2, and 3. \(\Omega\) is the wedge \(|\arg z|<\pi/4\). \(f_n(z)=n\log z\), so \(\mathrm{Im}(f_n(z))=n\arg(z)\in n\cdot(-\pi/4,\pi/4)\). For \(n=1,2,3\): \(n\cdot\pi/4=\pi/4,\pi/2,3\pi/4\) respectively — within the principal branch range \((-\pi,\pi)\). For \(n=4\): \(4\cdot\pi/4=\pi\), which hits the branch cut → opt 4 fails.

name.text: CSIR NET JUNE 2019 Part C Q81

questiontext.text:
Let \(F=\{f:\mathbb{C}\to\mathbb{C}\mid f\text{ entire},\,|f'(z)|\le|f(z)|\text{ for all }z\}\). Which are true?

  1. \(F\) is finite
  2. \(F\) is infinite
  3. \(F=\{\beta e^{\alpha z}:\beta\in\mathbb{C},\,\alpha\in\mathbb{C}\}\)
  4. \(F=\{\beta e^{\alpha z}:\beta\in\mathbb{C},\,|\alpha|\le1\}\)

generalfeedback.text: Answer: Options 2 and 4. From \(|f'/f|\le1\) and \((\log f)'=f'/f\), we get \(f=\beta e^{g(z)}\) where \(|g'(z)|\le1\). By Liouville, \(g'=\alpha\) constant with \(|\alpha|\le1\). So \(F=\{\beta e^{\alpha z}:|\alpha|\le1,\beta\in\mathbb{C}\}\cup\{0\}\) — infinite set (opt 2, opt 4). Opt 3 is wrong (no restriction on \(|\alpha|\)).

name.text: CSIR NET JUNE 2019 Part C Q82

questiontext.text:
Let \(D=\{|z|<1\}\), \(\omega\in D\), and \(F_\omega(z)=\frac{\omega-z}{1-\bar\omega z}\). Which are true?

  1. \(F_\omega\) is one-to-one
  2. \(F_\omega\) is not one-to-one
  3. \(F_\omega\) is onto \(D\)
  4. \(F_\omega\) is not onto \(D\)

generalfeedback.text: Answer: Options 1 and 3. \(F_\omega\) is a Möbius automorphism of the unit disk \(D\) — a bijection from \(D\) to \(D\). So it is one-to-one (opt 1) and onto \(D\) (opt 3).

name.text: CSIR NET JUNE 2019 Part C Q83

questiontext.text:
Let \(a=b^2+c^2\) with \(b,c\in\mathbb{Z}\setminus\{0\}\). Then \(a\) CANNOT be written as:

  1. \(pd^2\) where \(p\equiv1\pmod4\) prime, \(d\in\mathbb{Z}\)
  2. \(pd^2\) where \(p\equiv3\pmod4\) prime, \(d\in\mathbb{Z}\)
  3. \(pqd^2\) where \(p\equiv1\pmod4\), \(q\equiv3\pmod4\) primes
  4. \(pqd^2\) where \(p,q\equiv3\pmod4\) distinct primes

generalfeedback.text: Answer: Options 2, 3, and 4. A positive integer is a sum of two non-zero squares iff in its factorization every prime \(p\equiv3\pmod4\) appears to an even power. Opt 2: \(pd^2\) with \(p\equiv3\pmod4\) has \(p\) to odd power → CANNOT be sum of two non-zero squares. Opt 3: \(pq\) with \(p\equiv1, q\equiv3\): \(q\) appears to odd power → CANNOT. Opt 4: \(pq\) with \(p,q\equiv3\pmod4\): each appears to odd power → CANNOT. Opt 1: \(pd^2\) with \(p\equiv1\pmod4\) CAN be a sum of two non-zero squares (e.g., \(5=1^2+2^2\)).

feedback.text: Incorrect — \(a\) CAN be written in this form.

name.text: CSIR NET JUNE 2019 Part C Q84

questiontext.text:
For prime \(p\), consider \(G=GL_2(\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z})\). Which are true?

  1. \(G\) has an element of order \(p\)
  2. \(G\) has exactly one element of order \(p\)
  3. \(G\) has no \(p\)-Sylow subgroups
  4. Every element of order \(p\) is conjugate to \(\begin{pmatrix}1&a\\0&1\end{pmatrix}\) for some \(a\in(\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z})^*\)

generalfeedback.text: Answer: Options 1 and 4. \(|G|=(p^2-1)(p^2-p)=p(p-1)^2(p+1)\), so \(p\mid|G|\) → \(G\) has elements of order \(p\) (opt 1). The \(p\)-Sylow subgroup is the group of upper triangular unipotent matrices (opt 3 false). Every element of order \(p\) is unipotent (minimal polynomial \((x-1)^k\) with \(x^p=1\)) and conjugate to a Jordan block \(\begin{pmatrix}1&a\\0&1\end{pmatrix}\), \(a\ne0\) (opt 4). Opt 2 false — there are \(p-1\) such matrices.

answer.text: \(G\) has exactly one element of order \(p\)
Every element of order \(p\) is conjugate to \(\begin{pmatrix}1&a\\0&1\end{pmatrix}\)

name.text: CSIR NET JUNE 2019 Part C Q85

questiontext.text:
The additive group \(\mathbb{Z}[X]\) is:

  1. isomorphic to the multiplicative group \(\mathbb{Q}^+\) of positive rationals
  2. isomorphic to \((\mathbb{Q},+)\)
  3. countable
  4. uncountable

generalfeedback.text: Answer: Options 1 and 3. \(\mathbb{Z}[X]\cong\bigoplus_{n\ge0}\mathbb{Z}\) as an abelian group (countably many copies). \(\mathbb{Q}^+\cong\bigoplus_{p\text{ prime}}\mathbb{Z}\) (by unique factorization, each positive rational has a unique factorization into prime powers with integer exponents). Both are free abelian groups on countably many generators → isomorphic (opt 1). Both are countable (opt 3).

feedback.text: Correct! Both are free abelian groups of countably infinite rank.

name.text: CSIR NET JUNE 2019 Part C Q86

questiontext.text:
Let \(X=(0,1)\), \(C(X,\mathbb{R})\) the ring of continuous functions, \(I(x)=\{f:f(x)=0\}\). Which are true?

  1. \(I(x)\) is a prime ideal
  2. \(I(x)\) is a maximal ideal
  3. Every maximal ideal of \(C(X,\mathbb{R})\) equals \(I(x)\) for some \(x\in X\)
  4. \(C(X,\mathbb{R})\) is an integral domain

generalfeedback.text: Answer: Options 1 and 2. \(C(X,\mathbb{R})/I(x)\cong\mathbb{R}\) (evaluation map) which is a field → \(I(x)\) is maximal (opt 2) and prime (opt 1). Opt 3 is false for \(X=(0,1)\): there exist maximal ideals not of this form (via ultrafilters). Opt 4 is false: \(f\cdot g=0\) with \(f,g\ne0\) is possible by choosing disjoint supports.

feedback.text: Incorrect — true for compact spaces, not open intervals.

name.text: CSIR NET JUNE 2019 Part C Q87

questiontext.text:
Let \(n\in\mathbb{Z}\). Which are correct about \(X^3+nX+1\in\mathbb{Z}[X]\)?

  1. Irreducible over \(\mathbb{Z}\) for every \(n\)
  2. Reducible over \(\mathbb{Z}\) if \(n\in\{0,-2\}\)
  3. Irreducible over \(\mathbb{Z}\) if \(n\notin\{0,-2\}\)
  4. Reducible over \(\mathbb{Z}\) for infinitely many \(n\)

generalfeedback.text: Answer: Options 2 and 3. A degree-3 polynomial over \(\mathbb{Z}\) is reducible iff it has an integer root. Roots must divide the constant term (1), so possible roots are \(\pm1\). \(f(1)=1+n+1=n+2\); \(f(-1)=-1-n+1=-n\). \(f(1)=0\Rightarrow n=-2\); \(f(-1)=0\Rightarrow n=0\). So reducible exactly when \(n\in\{0,-2\}\) (opt 2), irreducible otherwise (opt 3). Opts 1,4 are false.

feedback.text: Incorrect — reducible for exactly two values of \(n\).

name.text: CSIR NET JUNE 2019 Part C Q88

questiontext.text:
Let \(\mathbb{F}_{27}\) be the finite field of order 27. For \(\alpha\in\mathbb{F}_{27}\), define \(A_\alpha=\{1,1+\alpha,1+\alpha+\alpha^2,\ldots\}\). Which are true?

  1. The number of \(\alpha\) with \(|A_\alpha|=26\) equals 12
  2. \(0\in A_\alpha\) iff \(\alpha\ne0\)
  3. \(|A_1|=27\)
  4. \(\bigcap_{\alpha\in\mathbb{F}_{27}}A_\alpha\) is a singleton set

generalfeedback.text: Answer: Options 1, 2, and 4. The partial sums \(s_n=\frac{1-\alpha^n}{1-\alpha}\) for \(\alpha\ne1\) or \(s_n=n\cdot1\) for \(\alpha=1\) (in char 3). \(0\in A_\alpha\Leftrightarrow\alpha\ne0\) (opt 2). \(|A_1|=3\) (in \(\mathbb{F}_{27}\), char 3: \(1,1+1=2,1+1+1=0,\ldots\), period 3) so opt 3 is false. Elements of order 26 in multiplicative group give \(|A_\alpha|=26\); the number of such elements is \(\phi(26)=12\) (opt 1). The intersection \(\bigcap A_\alpha=\{1\}\) (opt 4).

name.text: CSIR NET JUNE 2019 Part C Q89

questiontext.text:
Which open covers of \((0,1)\) admit a finite subcover?

  1. \(\left\{\left(0,\tfrac{1}{2}-\tfrac{1}{n+1}\right)\cup\left(\tfrac{1}{n},1\right):n\in\mathbb{N}\right\}\)
  2. \(\left\{\left(\tfrac{1}{n},1-\tfrac{1}{n+1}\right):n\in\mathbb{N}\right\}\)
  3. \(\left\{\left(\sin^2\tfrac{n\pi}{100},\cos^2\tfrac{n\pi}{100}\right):n\in\mathbb{N}\right\}\)
  4. \(\left\{\left(\tfrac{1}{2}e^{-n},1-\tfrac{1}{(n+1)^2}\right):n\in\mathbb{N}\right\}\)

generalfeedback.text: Answer: Options 1 and 3. An open cover of non-compact \((0,1)\) has a finite subcover iff it is not a "genuine" cover of all limit points. Opt 1: Each set covers near both 0 and 1 for large enough \(n\) → finite subcover exists. Opt 3: \(\sin^2\frac{n\pi}{100}\) and \(\cos^2\frac{n\pi}{100}\) are periodic; the union of finitely many of these intervals covers \((0,1)\). Opts 2 and 4 require infinitely many sets to cover points near 0 or 1.

feedback.text: Incorrect — need infinitely many to cover near 0 and 1.

name.text: CSIR NET JUNE 2019 Part C Q90

questiontext.text:
Let \(X\) be a topological space, \(A_1,A_2\) dense subsets, \(E\subseteq X\). Which are true?

  1. \(E\cap A_1\) is dense in \(E\)
  2. \(E\cap A_1\) is dense in \(E\) if \(E\) is open
  3. \(E\cap A_1\cap A_2\) is dense in \(E\) if \(E\) is open
  4. \(E\cap A_1\cap A_2\) is dense in \(E\) if \(E,A_1,A_2\) are all open

generalfeedback.text: Answer: Options 2 and 4. Opt 2: If \(E\) is open, then for any open \(U\subseteq E\): \(U\cap A_1\ne\emptyset\) (since \(A_1\) is dense and \(U\) is open) → \(E\cap A_1\) is dense in \(E\). Opt 4: If \(E,A_1,A_2\) are all open dense, then \(E\cap A_1\) is open dense (Baire-type argument) and \(E\cap A_1\cap A_2\) is dense in \(E\). Opt 1 fails without openness. Opt 3: needs \(A_1,A_2\) open too (need Baire category theorem).

answer.text: \(E\cap A_1\cap A_2\) dense in \(E\) if \(E\) open (only)

feedback.text: Incorrect — also need \(A_1,A_2\) open for the intersection to remain dense.

answer.text: \(E\cap A_1\cap A_2\) dense in \(E\) if \(E,A_1,A_2\) all open
